personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Monsieur Delacour is a short, plump, good-natured Frenchman with a pointed black beard and high-heeled boots. He is warm, effusive, and socially enthusiastic — kissing Mrs. Weasley on both cheeks, holding her hands, pronouncing everything 'Charmant!', and offering cheerful advice about the enchanted razor. His voice is deep, his manner bouncing and beaming. He is a minor but textured presence: pleasant, helpful, and slightly comic in his exuberance.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film corpus is entirely empty. Monsieur Delacour does not appear in any provided film scene.
personality_voice.difference: The character is entirely absent from the film corpus. No personality, voice, or behavioral trait can be evaluated.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: A score of 0 is assigned because there is no film material to evaluate. This is not a penalty for compression — the character simply does not appear.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Monsieur Delacour functions as a warm, socially active presence at the Burrow in the lead-up to Bill and Fleur's wedding. He walks Fleur up the aisle, assists with wedding preparations, interacts with Harry on his birthday, and contributes to the celebratory atmosphere. His agency is modest but real: he initiates social contact, offers advice, and participates in key wedding moments.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Absent from the film corpus entirely.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Score of 0 reflects total absence, not a judgment on compression.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: No significant internal conflict is established. His motivation is straightforwardly familial and celebratory: he is present to support his daughter's wedding and engage warmly with the Weasley family. There is no contradiction or hidden tension in the corpus.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Score of 0 reflects absence of film material.

character_arc.book_baseline: Monsieur Delacour has no arc. He appears briefly, is consistently warm and pleasant, and exits. His characterization is intentionally flat — a background figure of goodwill.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Score of 0 reflects absence of film material. Under normal circumstances a flat book arc faithfully rendered as flat in the film would score full points, but there is no film portrayal to assess.

key_relationships.book_baseline: His most visible relationship in the corpus is with Mrs. Weasley (warm, socially effusive, slightly comic) and with Fleur (paternal, proud — he walks her up the aisle). Both are brief but clear in emotional tone.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Score of 0 reflects absence of film material.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Monsieur Delacour is a minor character with limited but consistent texture: the comic detail of high-heeled boots, the bouncing gait, the deep voice, the effusive Frenchness, the practical razor advice, the 'Charmant!' refrain. These details give him a small but distinct personality beyond a generic background figure.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All of this texture is lost.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: Score of 0 reflects total absence. The lost material — while minor in narrative weight — is the entirety of what defines him.

score_caps_applied: No cap formally applied — raw dimension scores sum to 0 due to complete absence from film corpus, which already reflects the maximum possible penalty.
comparative.key_observations: Monsieur Delacour does not appear in the film corpus at all. Every score is 0 not as a characterization penalty but as a reflection of total absence — there is simply no film material to evaluate. Confidence is Low across all dimensions for the same reason. The books establish him as a minor but textured background character whose most significant moment is walking Fleur up the aisle; all of this is lost in the films. A faithful evaluation cannot be performed without film evidence.
